text:
UAVs (also known as Drones) can be used in a wide variety of public and civilian applications. Their usage is typically limited to scenarios where the risk to human life is limited or can be avoided. While multi-UAV systems lead to greater operational efficiency and lower operational costs than solo flights, context aware reliable connectivity in such systems remains a key challenge. Although MANETs and VANETs are two of the most widely studied research areas in the context of mobile ad hoc networks, the constraints and characteristics of such networks are rarely met in UAVs. Characteristics such as high-speed motion, varying levels of connectivity and highly dynamic topology, among others, are not sufficiently addressed by currently proposed solutions in the context of ad-hoc networks.

Ad hoc mesh topology is usually considered as the prime toplogy for such systems. However, research on the topology of multi-UAV networks is quite limited. Software Defined Networking (SDN) appears to be a highly suitable technology that enables easier network establishment, enhanced security and reliability. Routing in UAV networks must be thoroughly adapted from that used in Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs) and Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs) to account for highly dynamic environments, limited processing and transmission power resources of UAVs, varying radio link quality, high likelihood of node failure and consequently network partitions. Additional concerns, such as vertical handovers and energy efficient “green” networking must be addressed. This survey work therefore gives a state-of-the-art overview of current research on such aspects for multi-UAV networks. Challenges, existing research work and open research questions are described. Accordingly, suggestions for research directions are also provided.
